steps.title: Test the mutation timeline
steps.content: The researchers challenge the consensus that a genetic driver of smaller body size developed only within the last 20,000 years through selective breeding. Supporting evidence must show that the relevant body-size mutation existed earlier than that timeline.

steps.title: Reject weak genetic links
steps.content:
• Choice A - 'IGF1 has been isolated in genetic material from fossils more than 20,000 years old of the red wolf (Canis rufus) and certain other species related to dogs.': identifies the gene in older related species, but not the specific size-influencing mutation in a dog ancestor.
  • Choice B - 'An additional mutation related to IGF1 affects the development of characteristics other than body size in smaller dog breeds like bulldogs.': concerns another mutation and traits other than body size.
  • Choice D - 'One variant of IGF1 is found only in smaller dog breeds like bulldogs and appears to have emerged no more than 20,000 years ago.': supports the consensus timeline instead of undermining it.

steps.title: Use earlier body-size evidence
steps.content: Choice C - 'The mutation related to IGF1 influences body size and is found in 53,000-year-old genetic material from fossils of Siberian wolves (Canis lupus campestris), an ancestor of dogs.': places the body-size mutation in a dog ancestor long before 20,000 years ago, directly supporting the researchers' challenge. The answer is C.
